title: Next Greater Element
description:
Next Greater Element

Given a circular integer array nums (i.e., the next element of nums[nums.length - 1] is nums[0]), return the next greater number for every element in nums.

The next greater number of a number x is the first greater number to its traversing-order next in the array, which means you could search circularly to find its next greater number. If it doesn't exist, return -1 for this number.

Examples:

  • Input: nums = [1, 2, 1]
    Output: [2, -1, 2]
    Explanation: The next greater number for 1 is 2. The number 2 has no greater number. The next greater of the last 1 is 2 (wrapping around).
  • Input: nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 3]
    Output: [2, 3, 4, -1, 4]
  • Input: nums = [5, 4, 3, 2, 1]
    Output: [-1, 5, 5, 5, 5]
    Explanation: Every element except 5 wraps around and finds 5 as the next greater.

Constraints:

  • 1 <= nums.length <= 10000
  • -10^9 <= nums[i] <= 10^9
solution_text:
Next Greater Element

Algorithm:

  1. Initialize result array with -1 for all positions
  2. Use a monotonic decreasing stack storing indices
  3. Iterate through the array twice (to handle circular nature)
  4. For each element, pop indices from stack while current element is greater
  5. Set result for each popped index to current element

Time Complexity: O(n) — each element pushed and popped at most twice

Space Complexity: O(n) for stack and result array

Key Insights:

  • Traversing the array twice simulates the circular behavior
  • Use modulo to wrap indices: i % n
  • Only push indices from the first pass onto the stack (avoid duplicates)
  • Monotonic stack ensures we always find the next greater efficiently
verify:
def verify(actual_output, expected_output):
    passed = actual_output == expected_output
    output_str = str(actual_output)
    return [passed, output_str]
